вҖҷ who helped to rescue the Israeli people from their oppressors. It covers the times from their invasion of Canaan through to just before their first king. Most of these heroes or guides were actually warriors. Shimshon (commonly called вҖҳSamsonвҖҷ) is the best-known of all of them, and his story can be read in chapters 13вҖ“16.

From this document we can learn that Yisrael (вҖҳIsraelвҖҷ in older English translations) prospered when they obeyed YahwehвҖҷs instructions, but when they disobeyed, then God allowed other peoples and nations to oppress them. However, God is always ready and prepared to save his people whenever they turn away from their disobedience and return again to obeying the instructions that heвҖҷd given them.

The previous book was named after the main character Yehoshua (Joshua) and the following books (Ruth and 1 Shemuel/Samuel) are named after their main characters, but this document with somewhere around a dozen important characters, has no single prominent character that it could be named after. Instead itвҖҷs traditionally named вҖҳJudgesвҖҷ in English because of around twenty uses of the Hebrew root вҖҳЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӨЦ·ЧҳвҖҷ (shafat) often in roots normally translated as вҖҳjudgedвҖҷ. Despite that, thereвҖҷs not a single account in this document of these leaders judging the cases and conflicts of the common people. This dilemma offers two possible interpretations: 1/ that these leaders (often military leaders) did judge cases for the common people, but those details arenвҖҷt recorded, or 2/ (which weвҖҷve leaned more towards) that this Hebrew word has a wider range of meaning, and so weвҖҷve leaned towards the English word вҖҳguidedвҖҷ or вҖҳledвҖҷ in these contexts.
